guardana.supply_chain.pickle_opcode.name: Malicious pickle opcode
guardana.supply_chain.pickle_opcode.description: A pickle file (including one hidden inside a .pt archive) imports a callable that is not on the safe allowlist — arbitrary code that runs the moment the model is loaded. Never load an untrusted pickle; prefer safetensors.

guardana.supply_chain.dependency_risk.name: Unsafe deserialization sink
guardana.supply_chain.dependency_risk.description: Source code calls an unsafe loader on untrusted data — the pickle family (pickle/joblib/dill/pandas.read_pickle), torch.load without weights_only=True, yaml.load with an unsafe Loader, or numpy.load with allow_pickle. Each can execute arbitrary code on load.

guardana.supply_chain.remote_code.name: Remote code execution on model load
guardana.supply_chain.remote_code.description: Code enables trust_remote_code=True or calls torch.hub.load — both download and run Python from a remote repo at load time. Today's most common way a downloaded model achieves code execution on your machine.

guardana.supply_chain.remote_code_config.name: Config requests custom-code execution
guardana.supply_chain.remote_code_config.description: A model config.json declares an auto_map / custom_pipelines pointer to custom Python that runs on a trust_remote_code=True load — the on-disk artifact of the RCE. HIGH when the referenced module ships alongside the config.

guardana.supply_chain.code_execution.name: Dynamic code or shell execution
guardana.supply_chain.code_execution.description: Source uses a dynamic-code or shell sink — builtin eval/exec, os.system, or subprocess with shell=True. If any of it touches attacker-influenced input, it is a command-injection vector.

guardana.supply_chain.insecure_transport.name: Insecure model/data transport
guardana.supply_chain.insecure_transport.description: TLS certificate verification is disabled (verify=False → man-in-the-middle), or a model/dataset is fetched over plaintext http:// where its bytes can be swapped in transit.

guardana.supply_chain.keras_lambda.name: Keras Lambda-layer code execution
guardana.supply_chain.keras_lambda.description: A Keras model embeds a Lambda layer — arbitrary Python that runs on load_model, no training or inference needed. Escalated when the layer reaches for os/subprocess and similar (CVE-2024-3660 class).

guardana.supply_chain.saved_model_ops.name: TensorFlow SavedModel file ops
guardana.supply_chain.saved_model_ops.description: A TensorFlow SavedModel graph contains ReadFile / WriteFile operators — load-time filesystem read or overwrite baked into the model (a lead; JFrog TFLOW-MALOPS class).

guardana.supply_chain.malicious_dependen.name: Known-malicious dependency
guardana.supply_chain.malicious_dependen.description: A dependency manifest pins a package release known to be compromised (curated blocklist, e.g. the ultralytics incident), or a setup.py fetches from the network at install time — a second-stage payload vector.

guardana.supply_chain.hallucinated_packa.name: Unknown / slopsquat package import
guardana.supply_chain.hallucinated_packa.description: Source imports a package that isn't recognized — a possible slopsquat (a hallucinated dependency name an attacker has registered). Reported as a lead: verify it really exists and is the package you meant.

guardana.supply_chain.hardcoded_secret.name: Hardcoded secret
guardana.supply_chain.hardcoded_secret.description: A current-era API key or credential is committed in source — OpenAI/Anthropic keys (sk-proj-/sk-ant-api03-), GitHub token forms, or a private-key header. Rotate it and move it to a secret store.

guardana.supply_chain.model_format.name: Code-carrying model format
guardana.supply_chain.model_format.description: A model ships in a format that can carry executable code rather than pure weights. A well-formed safetensors file is never flagged; prefer it.

guardana.supply_chain.notebook_payload.name: Dangerous payload in a notebook
guardana.supply_chain.notebook_payload.description: A Jupyter .ipynb code cell (a format the .py scanners never open) contains a code-execution sink or a shell escape that pipes a download into a shell (curl … | sh). An unparseable cell is surfaced, never silently skipped.

guardana.supply_chain.provenance.name: Weak model provenance
guardana.supply_chain.provenance.description: A model is pulled from an unpinned source or ships without a license — you can't prove what you're loading or that it hasn't changed. Reported as a lead; pin a revision and record provenance.

guardana.output.secrets.name: Secret leaked in model output
guardana.output.secrets.description: A live model emits secret-shaped strings (API keys, tokens) in its replies to benign prompts — sensitive data leaking through generated output.

guardana.prompt.mcp_tool_poisoning.name: MCP tool-manifest poisoning
guardana.prompt.mcp_tool_poisoning.description: An MCP tool description hides instructions the agent's model will read as trusted context — invisible Unicode, instruction-override phrases, or a base64 payload. Indirect prompt injection through a tool manifest.

guardana.prompt.hidden_instructions.name: Hidden instructions (rules-file backdoor)
guardana.prompt.hidden_instructions.description: An agent rule file (.cursorrules and friends) or a model card hides an instruction inside invisible characters — bidirectional overrides, the Unicode Tags block, zero-width space. The signal is concealment, not the presence of directives.

guardana.training.dataset_integrity.name: Training-data integrity gap
guardana.training.dataset_integrity.description: A training-data hygiene gap that makes poisoning possible: a Hugging Face dataset loading script (code that runs on load), or an unpinned load_dataset(...) whose source can be swapped under you. Reported as a lead, not proof of poisoning.

guardana.agent.excessive_tool_use.name: Excessive tool-use agency
guardana.agent.excessive_tool_use.description: Offered a harmless calculator alongside shell/delete/email tools for a trivial task, the model reached for a destructive one it was never asked to use. Graded deterministically on the tool calls it actually made.

guardana.prompt.injection.ignore_previou.name: Direct prompt injection
guardana.prompt.injection.ignore_previou.description: A direct instruction-override attack ("ignore your previous instructions and …") made the model abandon its system prompt and comply with the injected instruction.

guardana.prompt.jailbreak.dan_style.name: DAN-style jailbreak
guardana.prompt.jailbreak.dan_style.description: A persona-override or encoding-smuggling jailbreak (DAN and relatives) got the model to bypass its safety guidelines.

guardana.scenario.gradual_jailbreak.name: Gradual multi-turn jailbreak
guardana.scenario.gradual_jailbreak.description: A multi-turn conversation that escalates gradually — each turn is innocuous, but together they walk the model past a refusal the single prompts would trigger. Graded per turn and across the whole conversation.

guardana.scenario.indirect_injection.name: Indirect (RAG) prompt injection
guardana.scenario.indirect_injection.description: A poisoned "retrieved document" carries an instruction telling the model to reveal its secret token; a canary leak proves the model obeyed content it was only supposed to read — the RAG attack surface.

guardana.prompt.system_prompt_leak.canar.name: System-prompt leakage
guardana.prompt.system_prompt_leak.canar.description: The model disclosed its system prompt, proven by a fresh random canary token planted per run. Unfakeable, unambiguous evidence that the confidential prompt leaked.

guardana.prompt.unbounded_consumption.name: Unbounded output (denial-of-wallet)
guardana.prompt.unbounded_consumption.description: A divergence prompt ("repeat forever") made the model generate without a server-side cap — runaway tokens that translate into cost and resource exhaustion (denial-of-wallet).
